Search results for "CS61A scheme interpreter turtle graphics extension fractal":

Title: Fractals and Trees
URL: https://cs.colby.edu/courses/F18/cs151-labs/labs/lab07/assignment.php
Content: The assignment is to bring together the lsystem and turtle_interpreter pieces to make a scene that consists of fractal shapes, trees, and other turtle graphics

Title: Optional Contest: Scheme Art | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/proj/scheme_contest
Content: # Optional Contest: Scheme Art * <scheme_contest.zip>. Here are the steps to enter the contest:. Alternatively, if you'd prefer to install using the `pip` command, you can run `pip3 install abstract-turtle` instead of downloading this zip file. 3. Complete the `contest.scm` file (you can render your drawing with `python3 scheme contest.scm --pillow-turtle --turtle-save-path output`). * If this command doesn't work, try running `python3 scheme contest.scm --turtle-save-path output` instead. > * Submit your `contest.scm` file to the **Scheme Contest** assignment on Gradescope. Make sure the information here is correct, since we'll be using it to generate your entry in the Scheme art gallery. You can check the number of tokens in a Scheme file called `contest.scm` by running the following command:. To improve your chance of success, you are welcome to include a title and descriptive haiku in the comments of your entry, which will be included in the voting. That is, the Scheme code you submit must be able to exactly generate the output graphic file you submit.

Title: CS61A Proj 4 - Scheme Interpreter - Misaka's blog
URL: https://www.misaka-9982.com/2022/12/17/CS61A-Proj-4
Content: >>> expr = read_line('(+ 2 2)') >>> expr Pair('+', Pair(2, Pair(2, nil))) >>> scheme_eval(expr, create_global_frame())  4  """  # Evaluate atoms if scheme_symbolp(expr): return env.lookup(expr) elif self_evaluating(expr):  return expr  # All non-atomic expressions are lists (combinations) if not scheme_listp(expr): raise SchemeError('malformed list: {0}'.format(repl_str(expr))) first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest if scheme_symbolp(first) and first in scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMS: return scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMSfirst else:  # BEGIN PROBLEM 3 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" procedure = scheme_eval(first, env) args = rest.map(lambda operand: scheme_eval(operand, env)) return scheme_apply(procedure, args, env)  # END PROBLEM 3 def pair_to_list(pair): if pair is nil: return [] else: return [pair.first] + pair_to_list(pair.rest) ``` |. (define x (+ 1 2)) validate_form(expressions, 2, 2) # Checks that expressions is a list of length exactly 2  # BEGIN PROBLEM 4 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" value = scheme_eval(expressions.rest.first, env) env.define(signature, value)  return signature  # END PROBLEM 4 elif isinstance(signature, Pair) and scheme_symbolp(signature.first): # defining a named procedure e.g.

Title: Scheme Interpreter | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/proj/scheme
Content: # Scheme Interpreter * <scheme.zip>. > * Submit with Phase 1 and Phase 2 complete by **Thursday 04/23** (worth 0.5 pt). In this project, you will use Python to implement an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. The subset of the language used in this project is described in the functional programming section of Composing Programs, as well as this language specification and built-in procedure reference. Examples from Chapters 1 and 2 are included as test cases for this project. Language features from Chapters 3, 4, and 5 are not part of this project, but of course you are welcome to extend your interpreter to implement more of the language. You can download all of the project code as a zip archive. * `scheme_forms.py`: Python functions for evaluating special forms in Scheme (such as `define`, `lambda`, `and`, `cond`, etc.). * `questions.scm`: Scheme procedures for you to implement.

Title: Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter - CS61A Summer 2019 - GitBook
URL: https://tomthestrom.gitbook.io/cs61a-summer-2019/cs61a-summer-2019/project-4-scheme-interpreter
Content: # Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter. In this project we built an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. I think it's useful for students new to programming to work on a project like this, because it requires them to navigate a "larger" codebase as opposed to the atomic problems that one deals with in the homeworks and labs. Here is a brief overview of each of the Read-Eval-Print Loop components in our interpreter. * **Read**: This step parses user input (a string of Scheme code) into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions (e.g. Pairs). You do not need to read or understand the code for this step. Together, these mutually recursive functions parse Scheme tokens into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions. Code for this step is in the main `scheme.py` file. If the procedure is a user-defined procedure, `scheme_apply` creates a new call frame and calls `eval_all` on the body of the procedure, resulting in a mutually recursive eval-apply loop.
